topic: Why people obey authority even when they should not — Milgram

script.title: Why You'd Obey an Order You Know Is Wrong

scenes.narration: In 1961, Stanley Milgram proved something terrifying. Ordinary people would administer what they believed were fatal electric shocks — simply because someone in a lab coat told them to.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a heavy metal switch panel with large lever, dramatic shadows cast across a concrete wall, ominous institutional setting

scenes.narration: Sixty-five percent of participants went all the way to 450 volts. Not monsters. Not sociopaths. Teachers, accountants, everyday people — just like you.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a dial turned to maximum, framed by deep shadows, a single harsh overhead light illuminating cracked institutional tiles

scenes.narration: The authority figure never touched anyone. He just wore a lab coat and said four words: 'Please continue the experiment.' That was enough.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: an empty authoritative chair behind a stark desk, a white coat hanging on a hook casting a long shadow across the floor

scenes.narration: When participants hesitated, they felt something Milgram called the 'agentic state' — a mental shift where you stop seeing yourself as responsible. You become an instrument of someone else's will.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a pair of hands gripping a table edge tightly, knuckles white, chains hanging loosely from wrists in dim light

scenes.narration: This is how atrocities happen. Not through armies of willing killers — but through ordinary people who outsource their conscience to whoever holds the title.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a long corridor of locked doors stretching into darkness, one bare bulb hanging at the far end, deep shadows swallowing the walls

scenes.narration: Milgram found that proximity mattered. When participants could see their victim — compliance dropped sharply. Distance makes cruelty easier. Always has.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a cracked mirror reflecting a distorted empty room, shards catching sparse light, the reflection showing only absence

scenes.narration: The question isn't whether you would obey. Research says most people would. The question is: what conditions have already made you someone else's instrument — without you realizing it?
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a marionette string casting a sharp shadow across a blank wall, the puppet absent — only the controlling wire remains

scenes.narration: Milgram didn't study monsters. He studied how normal minds surrender moral agency. His conclusion: the capacity for harm isn't rare. The conditions for it are everywhere.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: an empty institutional chair under a single spotlight, surrounded by absolute darkness, a single nameplate face-down on the floor
